summary: I was born in the United States while my parents were studying at the University of Maryland. After growing up in Ghana and working as a nurse there, I moved back to the United States in June 2011 for economic reasons. The process of transferring my nursing credentials to the US took about a year, but I finally became a registered nurse here in March 2017. Living and working in the United States represents opportunities and career advancement for many young professionals like me from Africa.

My parents were studying at the University of Maryland when I was born in the United States. After they graduated, we moved back to Ghana where I was raised. Although I did not attend the University of Maryland myself, it played a significant role in my early life as the place where my parents pursued their education.

I was raised in Ghana after my parents and I moved back there following their graduation. I studied nursing at the University of Ghana and worked in various hospital departments, including medical, surgical, oncology, and the operating room. Malaria was the most common illness we treated, and the healthcare system was challenging due to high patient-to-nurse ratios and a cash-and-carry system. I worked as a registered nurse in Ghana for about one and a half years before moving to the United States in 2011 for better economic opportunities.

After high school, I wanted to study economics but my math grades weren't sufficient, so I chose nursing at the University of Ghana. I completed a four-year nursing program there, passed the nursing exam, and started working in hospitals. The University of Ghana was where I gained my foundational nursing education that prepared me for my career both in Ghana and later in the United States.

During my nursing work in Ghana, malaria was the dominant illness we treated. We had a very high patient-to-nurse ratio, and many patients came in with malaria, among other diseases like cardiac issues and diabetes. Treating malaria was a significant part of my nursing experience in Ghana’s hospitals.

When I moved to the United States, I settled in Virginia, near Washington, D.C., where some of my cousins lived. After passing the nursing exam and becoming a registered nurse in the US, I started the process of transferring my registration to Virginia so I could begin working there. Virginia represents my new professional home as I continue my nursing career in the US.

I moved to the United States and lived near Washington, D.C., in Virginia where my cousins were based. This area became my initial home in the US as I worked to transfer my nursing credentials and prepare for the licensing exam. Washington, D.C. was part of my support network during this transition period.

The Nursing Board of California evaluated my academic credentials from Ghana to determine if I was eligible to take the US nursing licensure exam. This process involved submitting transcripts and documents, which was challenging because obtaining them from Ghana took time and effort. After their evaluation, they found me eligible to sit for the NCLEX exam, which I passed in March 2017.

The NCLEX was the nursing licensure exam I had to pass to become a registered nurse in the United States. It was quite different from the Ghanaian nursing exam, focusing on multiple-choice questions where you select the most accurate answer, including detailed knowledge of medications and their effects. After a year of preparation, I passed the NCLEX in March 2017 and officially became a registered nurse in the US.
